text:
Baldur's Gate 3 isn’t just a great RPG—it’s the kind of game that makes most other RPGs feel slightly unfinished afterward.

At its core, it’s a **turn-based, party-based CRPG set in the Dungeons & Dragons universe**, where almost everything is driven by choice: combat, dialogue, exploration, and even failure. The game constantly reacts to what you do in ways that feel genuinely handcrafted rather than scripted. That level of reactivity is a big reason it’s been called one of the best RPGs ever made.

The biggest strength is how much **player freedom and agency** it gives you. You can solve problems through combat, stealth, persuasion, absurd physics, or just creative nonsense the devs somehow accounted for. It feels less like “following a questline” and more like *actually playing a tabletop campaign with a very ambitious DM*.

Combat is turn-based but extremely flexible. Positioning, abilities, environmental interactions, and party synergy all matter, and even fights you “mess up” can turn into memorable chaos instead of just a reload moment.

And then there’s the writing and characters. The companions are a huge highlight—well-voiced, deeply written, and reactive to your decisions in ways that actually change relationships and outcomes. It’s one of those rare games where you end up caring about your party way more than expected.

But it’s not flawless.

The biggest barrier for some players is the **sheer complexity and pacing**. The game is dense. Systems stack on top of systems (spells, status effects, gear interactions, dialogue checks), and it can feel overwhelming if you’re not used to CRPGs or D&D-style rules. Even fans admit it can feel like “homework” managing everything at times.

There’s also the reality that the game is so big that you can easily miss entire storylines or regions without realizing it. That freedom is impressive, but it can also mean uneven pacing depending on how you play.

And while later acts are still strong, some players note occasional performance dips or rougher pacing compared to the near-perfect opening.

Still, none of that really changes the overall picture.

With **overwhelmingly positive Steam reviews (around 96%+ positive across hundreds of thousands of players)**, it’s clear how widely it’s loved and how impactful it’s been.

**Pros:**

* Insane player freedom and reactivity
* Deep, tactical combat with endless creativity
* Incredible writing and memorable companions
* Huge amount of content and replayability
* Choices actually matter in meaningful ways

**Cons:**

* Very steep learning curve (especially for newcomers)
* Can feel overwhelming or slow to manage
* Easy to miss content due to branching structure
* Some pacing/performance issues in later sections

**Verdict:**
A landmark RPG that sets a new standard for choice-driven storytelling and reactive game design. It’s not always simple, but it’s almost always impressive.

**10/10**

mentions.evidence: Insane player freedom and reactivity
You can solve problems through combat, stealth, persuasion, absurd physics, or just creative nonsense the devs somehow accounted for.
Combat is turn-based but extremely flexible.
The companions are a huge highlight

text:
I was on the fence about getting this game for so long despite it being the main reason i got a pc, because i don't play games with turn based combat all that much and i saw a lot of people say that the combat style can be pretty difficult to learn. I decided recently to get it and i have no regrets.
 The character writing is great (plus they're hot so bonus), the map is huge, there's so many different choices there's literally no way that you can uncover everything in one playthrough, you have the option to be genuinely evil which i think is cool, the romances obviously are great, its multiplayer so you can play with friends, they have an in game mod downloader so you don't HAVE to go looking all over the place if you want some mods, and the base game is amazing on its own though i honestly haven't even downloaded mods yet. The game is just overall great, and I'm for sure going to be doing multiple playthroughs.
The turn based combat really isn't as hard to learn as people make it seem, the more difficult part it finding out which moves, spells, and characters to use and take in your party. But its totally learnable especially with the story mode difficulty. 
Honestly love this game so much

mentions.evidence: The turn based combat really isn't as hard to learn as people make it seem
The character writing is great
the map is huge, there's so many different choices there's literally no way that you can uncover everything in one playthrough

text:
i cant speak much on the lore, im not a massive tolkein nerd. I will say I really do like gollum and I wanted this game despite the fact i knew most people felt it wasn't very good. I got it 80% off, so i figured it was worth it. 5 Hours in, ive rage quit twice because the controls are a bit finnicky and the AI threshold is honestly quite stupid. The way some of the stealth missions are set up is extremely annoying, as you cannot automatically sneak coming off a ladder or ledge, so often times the enemy will hear you and alert everyone else. even if you manage to get away, they'll still be standing around and shouting like they can still see you. They also spot you through the smallest holes ever. I, however, am a masochist, and like to torture myself, and will do so for my little snot monster gollum.
Some good things: the graphics are honestly quite beautiful. coming off of games like bg3 and elden ring, I;d say the backgrounds are similarly detailed and a feast for the eyes (though i would not say they compete), however the character models are the UGLIEST things ive seen in a bit. Gollum looks like a bug-eyed cocomelon kid and the only nice looking npc ive seen is the candle-man, all the other ones are frankly scaring me lmao. this is not to say gollum should be cute or normal looking, he is a filthy cave creature after all, but idk..he just looks off.
If youre considdering buying the game, buy it on this mega spring sale and dont bother paying 40 bucks for it. Prepare for lots of stealth and parkour sequences.
Edit: the game still sucks lmao

text: I want the $0 I paid for this back.

Below was my reason for a refund statement, I stand by it:

I don't even know where to begin. A 30fps that dips when you do just about anything even on a computer that can run all other new games without issue is borderline offensive. The game is filled with glitches, it already crashed on me (ohh and the black screen counted as "game-play" time), it's so poorly optimized even though with the cartoon looking graphics one would think it wouldn't be so taxing to run. I cannot believe this comes from the same studio that produced Dishonored and Prey, a $70 price tag for this heap is offensive. So incredibly disappointed in Bethesda and Arkane. The game looks and feels completely unfinished, what is the point of a so-called open world game when one is able to interact with almost nothing? If this game were around thirty dollars I may not bother with a refund, but the price point for the impressively poor quality game is a travesty. No one should even be able to purchase this game through Steam until some major updates happen, where's the accountability?

mentions.evidence: A 30fps that dips when you do just about anything even on a computer that can run all other new games without issue is borderline offensive.

text:
I replayed through the entire trilogy after around 13 years or so and boy, it left a hole in me the same way it did when I was 13 years old. These are some of my favourite quotes towards the end of the trilogy that I think pretty much summarises how ME as a franchise made me feel back then & even now.


"Stand in the ashes of a trillion dead souls and ask the ghosts if honor matters. 

The silence is your answer." 
-Javik, The Last Prothean

Garrus: "There's an old saying here on Earth--may you be in heaven half an hour before the devil knows you're dead. Not sure if Turian heaven is the same as yours, but, if this thing goes sideways and we both end up there--meet me at the bar. I'm buying."

Shepard: "Goodybye, Garrus. And if I'm up there in that bar, and you're not... I'll be looking down. You'll never be alone."

There's a lot more like the last exchange between Tali and Shepard (mind you that exchange truly kills me too).

mentions.evidence: it left a hole in me the same way it did when I was 13 years old

Mass Effect Legendary Edition is a fantastic way to experience one of the best sci-fi trilogies ever made. The remaster looks great, plays smoothly, and makes replaying the whole series feel fresh all over again. Great characters, great story, great choices — this is one of those games that just keeps pulling you back in. The hours don't reflect my previous play through of the game on the original versions.

If you have never experienced the universe of Mass Effect, catch the legendary edition on sale, you will not regret it.

Back when the name Bioware used to mean something we got this awesome trilogy  of space-opera rpg games that are unsurpassed even today. 
I first played this on Xbox 360 at my local Internet café when  was just a kid and it stayed with me through all these years. Now I have replayed it with the same Shepard  I did back then to relieve one of the greatest stories ever told in gaming.
Today's Bioware could never make something like this and it is clear why they have fallen, their games from almost 2 decades ago are better and more impactful then anything they put out recently.

text: Such a bad game 1/10

Do not buy this game if you don't want to turn on secure boot on your system. 

CSM (one of the things you need to turn on) destroys your performance, and trying to turn on secure boot has the risk of bricking your entire system if you ♥♥♥♥ something up in the process.
I've wanted to play this game since it came out, but EA's stupid decisions make it not worth it in the long run. 
I'm literally unable to play, since Javelin refuses to notice that secure boot is on, so It's a waste of money.

I also forgot to mention that if your motherboard doesn't support secure boot, you can still ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ play the game for some reason??? It literally defeats the purpose of "using secureboot to help protect against cheating" if you let people play anyway if they can't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ enable secureboot. You can also get around secureboot and cheat anyway, as people have done time and time again in this game and in BF6 (from what i've heard from friends and other people i've talked to about this)

Also it's EA and they've got a shady past and are a pretty ♥♥♥♥♥♥ company, so this is all expected. 


TLDR: Don't buy if you don't want to enable secureboot and lower the performance of your system, and be aware that it does literally nothing to prevent cheating.

text:
After hitting 100% completed on this game I can say in my opinion overall its a 5/10 game. 

The games start and mid game was very fun building up your tavern and managing the customers and stock levels was very enjoyable. Along with some other features that had potential (such as the adventurer table and brewery) but just didn't expand on the very basic setup it had. 

After around 7 hours of playing though the game pretty much just an afk simulator and after 100% this game I have no real interest in playing though a second time. In the end I ended up skipping the days to get though the very tedious quest system to progress though the tracker. Overall though its probably still worth the price if its on a discount. Took 15 hours to 100%.

text:
This game was fun, but I found the research and quest aspects to be broken. I played for 14 hours before I unlocked the garden, but it didn't expand my buildable area- I had already made all that into Inn, duh! So I didn't make a garden. Many of the researches were very so-so- rewards, with some being just seemingly nothing or confusing.  When you hire a new person they are assigned permanently to the new kitchen or bar you built, great, but also assigned to every floor??? Ahhh!

All in all, I enjoyed this game. I think it is find for achievement hunting, look at my blue ribbon, wooo~! But I think it was a grind and not super fun to get there. I felt like once I got to 10 hours I had built my tavern and really enjoyed it, but there was little to do. Money piled up, workers started making 100 gold a day, I had like 300 researchers before I realized the hard stop of needing guests to come in. Then I deleted half my hotel rooms and made more tables.

In the end, I think they wanted a balanced game and it sort of isn't. All the same, I enjoyed it and it was easy to play. There were very few "ugh ERMAHGERD" moments with the building- everything can snap into place, etc. 

there was a lot of attention to certain details, which was so funny to me- like the people in the bathroom get pixelated. So respectful! I laughed. This game is cute, you'll like it.

mentions.evidence: I found the research and quest aspects to be broken

they are assigned permanently to the new kitchen or bar you built, great, but also assigned to every floor??? Ahhh!
I think it is find for achievement hunting, look at my blue ribbon, wooo~!

text:
(played mostly on PS4)

the villians are incredibly shallow and everyone treats them like insane social commentary. sexual assault bad = standard take, kamoshida bad = life-changing revelation about the relations between susceptible women and powerful men. i genuinely don't even remember half the villains because they don't necessarily even hold any ideals, aside from Maruki who i'd consider more of anti-hero than a villain. SMT at least expresses ideals that can be reasoned with, Persona 5 is just "let's beat up the rapist and act like this is shocking commentary". it's easily the most lackluster game in the entire extended series in expressing what it really wants to be, it's just a classic good guy vs bad guy until you (situationally) face off with Maruki. the game doesn't delve into especially novel or controversial angles on power dynamics; it presents an extreme, villainous example and lets the player feel righteous defeating it. powerful bad people bad > teenagers with magic fix it > society cheers.

i especially hate how the phantom thieves are designed as this chaotic, immature group, but their behavior is almost entirely in-line with the law routes of other games (this person is evil > we change their heart > society conforms to our moral judgment). by portraying them as the good guys, it kinda just invalidates the chaos routes in other games because they offer no option to be so unambiguously righteous. it just feels like watching Death Note again, without the moral qualm of deciding whether or not the protagonist's questionable actions are actually good or not, the player is almost never asked to seriously doubt the righteousness of the core formula. even when society turns against them mid-story, the narrative ultimately vindicates them rather than forcing a philosophical reckoning; all the while, you're harboring a cat who incessantly lusts over and makes incredibly sexual and inappropriate comments towards a female party member for the entirety of the game, to the point that he endangers another individual to make the phantom thieves jealous, and lo-and-behold, the phantom thieves just so happen to catch up with them and it's all okay. Morgana never changes, he never stops being a creep, he never stops making comments towards Ann or belittling Ryuji, but it's okay because he's one of the good guys.

mentions.evidence: the villians are incredibly shallow
it's easily the most lackluster game in the entire extended series in expressing what it really wants to be

text: It's not a terrible game, this game has been compared to cyberpunk but cyberpunk doesn't take place in space (unless you did the sun ending), and the developers aren't worried about minor details like how the one nightclub in the entire game is set up when the rest of Neon looks fine, on top of the fact they also had to account for the rest of the planets in all of the many systems. But enough rambling, all I'm saying is I spent 30 bucks on Outer Worlds for the same experience and all I got was pretty much Bioshock 1 in space, all respect to Bioshock 1, to me it seemed like Obsidian straight up copied their formula. In this game however I have the freedom to go to any planet, reset myself in a different universe all together, romance my companions, and fly any ship I want. It's a good SPACE game with a ton of freedom and that's why I recommend it.

text: Rocket League is one of those games that's impossible to put down — I've sunk hundreds of hours into it and still find myself coming back, chasing that perfect aerial or that one clean redirect that makes everything click. The gameplay itself is genuinely brilliant: simple enough to pick up in minutes, but the ceiling on skill is basically infinite, and every goal you score feels earned through pure mechanics rather than luck. That said, it's hard to ignore what Epic did to this game — removing trading, gutting the progression system, and turning what used to be a thriving community into a cash-grab storefront was a gut punch to longtime fans. The toxicity in lobbies is real too, though the quick chat 'Nice shot!' after an own goal somehow never gets old. Despite all that, the core game is still one of the best ever made, and I'd recommend it to anyone who wants a competitive experience unlike anything else — just temper your expectations around everything outside the actual matches.

text: I feel like God

It scratches an itch. Bannerlord have a style of game that could be the sweet spot between Civ style world/kingdom building with a mix of Total War battles. However the execution is verging on poor. 

Vanilla game is okay, its not great. If you want to have any kind of fun then you will absolutely need mods as the base systems and features just arent there. Mods for diplomacy, larger battles and armies are a must.

The positives are; 
- you can play the game multiple different ways (Merc, Trader, Vassel, Ruler) and its completely open as to how you want your character to play out, it doesnt force you into a specific type. 
- The world, for the most part, moves pretty consistantly and things constantly change, kingdoms lose towns and castles and armies declare war on you out of the blue.  
- Battles can be fun and do require some thinking, especially if you find yourself outnumbered. 
- Seiges are a great feature, you just lack the ability to control them well.
- The kingdoms are well thought out and styled and the database that is there could very easily be expanded upon and made to be more interactive.

Cons are; 
- Dialouges are rough and uninteresting, every NPC interaction is identical and repetitive. 
- AI lords and leaders are terrible when it comes to tactics, both in seiges and in battles.
- Wars are persistent and sometimes seem pointlessly invoked and when this happens continuously as you are trying to rebuild or work on other things it came become incredibly frustrating. 
- Graphics are very poor. 
- There is so much potential in the towns and villages if they were more interactive.
- Hideouts are bland and monotonous. 

Overall the potential was huge, but its a basic rendition and for a while it is fun. I would recommend simply for the joy of the first playthrough, after that the cracks in the game start to spoil the overall enjoyment. Buy on discount

No Man's Sky game has plenty to offer - procedurally generated planets (including weather, terrain, etc.), animals, plants, encounters, etc. It even has base building, freighters and expeditions, and settlement management. But it's unfortunately a mile wide and an inch deep. It tries to do everything, but doesn't do any one thing better than any other game. 

From the moment you leave the starting planet, the game is an exercise in tedium and repetition. I spent 60 hours in this game. I continuously wondered what the point of all of it was. I was never challenged. I never felt a sense of danger. I never had epic moments of wonder or of adventure. 

I feel like I could forgive its monotony and incredible grind if either: I felt I was in a universe with other people, or the UI wasn't so damn dreadful. 

The multiplayer aspects of this game are totally unnecessary. I never fought off pirates beside a random person, nor had to group up with someone to take on a difficult mission. The moments I did interact with others, it was passing by someone in the corridor of the Anomaly or looking at their ship as they came to land in the same place.  Think of Destiny's Tower system, but with more steps between NPCs. 

And I've been saving the worst part for last: the UI. It is genuinely one of the worst user interfaces I've ever used in a game. Sure, it has a quest log and a compendium to track your progress, it has inventories, it has customisation to your exosuit or ship or your tools. Even the crafting menus exist. But that's all that can be said positively about the UI. 

There are ZERO shortcuts. Nearly EVERYTHING requires you to hold a key down for a second, and everything has an animation that you cannot skip or speed up. And you do this several thousand times in your playthrough. Landing a ship? Yep, you have to watch the same animation everytime. Teleporting to a settlement? Yep, hold TAB to skip the forced window that comes up telling you what planet you're on. Opening a box? Hold on, before you take what's in the box, you have to first aim way above it because the icon conflicts with switching off a random light in the middle of a barren wasteland, and then you hold a button to complete an arbitrary second-long interaction before the animation starts and you have to remove the random junk from the front of it so you can then persevere through another animation and a mandatory 2 second waiting period. THEN you will be given an item that you will ignore for your entire playthrough. 

And the UI constantly overrides itself. If I pin a quest, stop overriding it just because I entered a different star system and got attacked by pirates that was entirely out of my control. If I pin a quest, stop overriding it just because I'm holding a map that I received as a quest reward from my last quest. If I pin a quest, stop overriding it just because I accidentally complete a random side objective by using my mining laser on an animal that accidentally stepped in front of me. If I already have the items in my inventory when I pick up a quest, PLEASE for the love of god, let me hand it in immediately - I don't want to have to teleport or fly offworld just to give the game time to realise that I have what I need in my inventory. And stop giving me quest rewards like recipes/blueprints that I already unlocked because I couldn't be bothered waiting for the quest NPC to recognise I had the items in my inventory all along.

The UI is INCREDIBLY frustrating. I think I spent 18 of my 60 hours in this game in animations I couldn't skip, interactions I couldn't make instantaneous, and dialogue that I couldn't skip immediately. 

Overall, No Man's Sky is fine. Someone will enjoy it. But for me, it was mediocre at best and frustratingly tedious at worst.

mentions.evidence: No Man's Sky game has plenty to offer
it's unfortunately a mile wide and an inch deep. It tries to do everything, but doesn't do any one thing better than any other game.

W GAME

i lowkey didn't think i'd like this game, and i hadn't really don't any research at all before buying it. for reference i had gotten it confused with terraria (which i knew nothing about aside from the fact people compare it to minecraft), so i thought id try a new genre of game and have maybe a two week phase of playing, but i was VERY pleasantly surprised. i bought it before going to a friends house and waited until i was there to play, and we both loved it so much i influenced her to get it herself after i went home. (we didn't play coop because she didn't have the game, so one of us did the mouse and the other did the controls lol) every day after that i had legit played a season a day and skipped school to play as soon as i woke up. this game is so goated. i play the sims 4 sometimes, mostly for the CAS and building part of the game, so i was familiar with mods. mods add a whole new level of customization and options to how you want to play, but i hadn't felt the need to install many mods at all. the game itself is just that good. the only mods i had gotten were ones that change the colors/shades in the game to a less neon variant and a mod that adds a little more dialogue to one of the bachelors. it runs really well even with mods so if you're worried about that, coming from someone writing this from a crappy laptop and who has internet that takes an hour to download a single gigabyte, it shouldn't be a concern at all. i'm not usually one to try and 100% complete games and minmax everything whatsoever, but all of the components to the game (skills/earning money/maxing friendships/completing collections/whatever else i haven't mentioned) genuinely gives me such a dopamine hit i now feel the need to completely finish the game to its fullest potential. it's definitely possible even if you aren't the best at games, there are so many tutorials and tips out there if you need them, and whenever i'm playing the stardew valley wiki (my goat) is legit always open. buy this game if you want to have a game like minecraft to play with 100x the dopamine rush and gambling factors (in a positive way)

What's not to love about this game, from the characters, to the tasks, to the visuals you get; theres always something that'll catch your eye.
Of course this game is always expanding and there is always room to grow, so adding things like more character development (Wizard, Dwarf, Krobus, etc.), maybe a new NPC or 2, or even a chance for some better in game mechanics - such as being able to save without waiting for the end of the day.

At the end of the day this game is cute, fun and such a wonderful way to unwind after a long day of being an adult - I'd recommend this game to everyone I know!

Many of the user-reviews for this game are [i] very [/i] positive, and e.g. emphasizes the emotional impact and wisdom of this game. I read some of those reviews and bought the game with 75% discount, which I think is a price worth the experience I had with the game. Had I payed the full price of the game, I would probably have been left with a feeling of disappointment. 

Nonetheless, I did enjoy playing the game! Very nice atmosphere and music. The story is okay, and it touches an important area of our existence, which is death. I think this game feels more like a movie compared to other story-rich games. 

I would recommend buying this game, but only on discount.

        [td]Lubuntu Linux 14.04 LTS (Trusty Tahr)[/td]
    [/tr]
[/table]

--------

Instead jumping, you change the gravity; instead confront enemies, avoid them. This is the premise of VVVVVV, a simple game, but very fun! The colored graphics, like a Commodore 64 game, is nice and can bring a sense of nostalgia for those who played these old games. The soundtrack is spectacular, an agradable 8-bit sound for the ears.

In the main game itself is not large, perhaps, as were the classic side action games, however, it will spend a few more hours if you are hunting achievements. This can be the negative criticism for some players, they are almost impossible - if you are patient, a bit of training and soon you can, because has a pattern for beat the levels.

The controller compatibility works very well, in my case, I used a Xbox 360 controller.

mentions.evidence: Instead jumping, you change the gravity; instead confront enemies, avoid them. This is the premise of VVVVVV, a simple game, but very fun!

text:
Fun game, very much a sequel to Wasteland 2 and the original Fallouts.
Visually quite good, as far as isometric turn based rpg strat games go.
Almost all dialogue has quality voice over for them.

Also, if you so desire; There is a giant statue of Ronald Reagan controlled by a 100 year old AI version of Reagan, which you can upgrade to fight off raiders, and oh boy, try not to bring up the commies. Triggers poor ol' Ronald.

Minor complaints:
The game is a bit short, without the DLCs you've got about 30 hours from start to finish.
On higher difficulty settings, if you don't properly plan out your character level ups, you can end up with a real hard end game, although conversely there are some cookie cutter OP builds.
Most of the difficulty comes from sheer chance on your to hit and resist rolls, besides using cover, there is very little actual strategy in combat, that doesn't really change from fight to fight.
Steeltown DLC adds some crappy new stacking status effects, that are more annoying than interesting to play around with.
Cult of the holy detonation DLC seems to be a bit lacking in stuff to do, like they wanted to implement more but cut a bunch in the end.

10/10 Reagan lives again, My wasteland ride is basically night-rider on steroids, and the hippy robot commune must die!

mentions.evidence: Visually quite good
Almost all dialogue has quality voice over for them.
The game is a bit short, without the DLCs you've got about 30 hours from start to finish.
if you don't properly plan out your character level ups, you can end up with a real hard end game, although conversely there are some cookie cutter OP builds
Most of the difficulty comes from sheer chance on your to hit and resist rolls, besides using cover, there is very little actual strategy in combat, that doesn't really change from fight to fight.
Steeltown DLC adds some crappy new stacking status effects, that are more annoying than interesting to play around with.

text: Super fun game that plays like a mix of Xcom and Fallout. The combat and squad building/RPG elements are quite fun, as is the overall gameplay. Music and sound are top notch and usually appropriate for the mood. Some of the characters in the game are quite wild and most quests and places you come across are pretty interesting. The story is good, but unfortunately the writing is very poor. Plot points sometimes disappear, results of some dialogue choices make no logical sense, and apparently there's never any way to change any outcome, as if once something happens, it's final and no one will ever think of a solution around it. Factions are almost entirely useless, as there's only a few and once you do a few quests with them, they're largely forgotten until the end game credits. Kinda wish there was more content and quests with existing factions/cities that would trigger as you progressed into the game, instead of things being left behind once done. Also wish your squad mates had more dialogue with each other, as the few times Lucia or Kwon added commentary during the game, it was very welcome.

Pros: Fun and beautiful game, with insane details and a very atmospheric world filled with wonderful views. Every exploration feels unique and not repetitive — there is always a new challenge and something unexpected. At the beginning, some people might find it difficult to remember all key bindings for different actions, but with time it becomes easier.

I was never a big fan of this type of game, but I fully enjoyed it. I was immersed in the gameplay, and time flew by. The tombs and crypts are very beautiful, and the lighting effects in this game are absolutely on point.

Cons: you may need the search on youtube on how to pass a level or 2 or 3 but with time you get better at them :) , also voice acting and conversation could have been better .

Overall — 9/10
Value for Money — 10/10
Recommended: Yes

text:
[h1]A must-play for Tomb Raider fans![/h1]

[b]Graphics & Visuals:[/b] The graphics are much better than the previous games, and Lara has a more mature look.

[b]The Jungle Environment:[/b] About 90% of the game is set in jungles. It feels so realistic that you might actually get lost!

[b]Story & Gameplay:[/b] The story and gameplay are a bit weaker compared to the earlier titles.

[hr][/hr]
[b]Final Verdict:[/b] Despite some weak points, I highly recommend this game to all Tomb Raider and gaming enthusiasts.

text: nice game

I bought this game on a crazy sale. I think it's worth it. I bought it despite having it on my Xbox game pass. I just wanted to play it on PC because the graphics are awesome. I absolutely love the cutscenes; I don't get the hate. While there is a lack of action, I do think the action, while short, is awesome in itself. Especially the ones against the creepy guys in the cenote.

This game came out in 2018, I think, and it should get a price decrease at this point. Nevertheless, if this game is on sale, it's a 10/10 PURCHASE, and I would absolutely recommend it to anyone who loves mystery and exploration games.

text: Get noita'd
horrible

I've never been as conflicted about a game as I am with [i]Infinite[/i].

I played both [i]Bioshock 1 & 2[/i] in about two days, binging them nonstop because I thought they were both fantastic. The game mechanics, storylines, characters, everything. 

Then along came [i]Infinite[/i].

Over the years, there had definitely been a divide among fans I'd found on whether or not this is a good game. Some people insisted and swore this one was the best in the entire series, while others argued it barely felt it lived up to the [i]Bioshock[/i] name. 

Personally, I'm in the middle. It's fun, but for some reason it just doesn't feel like a part of the series. Some people say it's because it's set in the sky, not in Rapture but I'm not sure what it is. 

No matter what, it's worth a play for sure.
